county_metadata.record_losses: 1855 courthouse fire at Arkansas Post destroyed many early records; county seat moved to DeWitt 1855
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Arkansas County formed 1813 as one of the original five counties of Arkansas Territory, originally encompassing most of the territory. County seat was at Arkansas Post (site of the earliest permanent European settlement in the lower Mississippi Valley, founded by Henri de Tonti in 1686). The county seat moved to DeWitt in 1855 after a courthouse fire. Arkansas Post was a major French, Spanish, and American military and trading post.
county_metadata.research_tips: Arkansas County is the oldest county in the state, originally encompassing nearly all of Arkansas Territory. Arkansas Post, the first permanent European settlement in the lower Mississippi Valley (1686, French), is within this county. The county seat moved from Arkansas Post to DeWitt after the 1855 fire. The Arkansas History Commission in Little Rock holds surviving territorial-era records. Check the Arkansas Post National Memorial archives for French and Spanish colonial records. Land records at the County Clerk's office in DeWitt begin after the 1855 fire; earlier land transactions may be reconstructed from General Land Office (GLO) patent records at the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) website. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving deed books. The county's location at the confluence of the Arkansas and Mississippi Rivers makes Freedmen's Bureau records and plantation records essential for African American research.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Arkansas County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in DeWitt holds deed books from 1823

source_profiles.survival_notes: Early deed books damaged or destroyed; 1855 courthouse fire at Arkansas Post destroyed many early records; county seat moved to DeWitt 1855. Post-loss records at County Clerk's office are generally complete

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Ashley County formed 1848 from Chicot, Drew, and Union counties. County seat is Hamburg. Located in the southeastern corner of Arkansas in the Cotton Belt. Significant timber resources led to a lumber industry in the late 1800s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ashley County records are held at the County Clerk's office in Hamburg, with good survival from its 1848 formation. This Cotton Belt county in southeastern Arkansas has strong enslaved persons records in estate inventories and probate files. The Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies of early deed books. Check Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA for post-Civil War African American families. Ashley County's proximity to Louisiana means some families appear in both states' records. The Hamburg Library maintains local history files and cemetery transcriptions. FamilySearch has indexed many Ashley County vital records. Land patents from the GLO are available online through BLM. Church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations provide baptism and burial data from the mid-1800s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Ashley County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Hamburg holds deed books from 1848

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Baxter County formed 1873 from Fulton, Izard, Marion, and Searcy counties. County seat is Mountain Home. Located in the Ozark Mountains region of north-central Arkansas. The county's economy was subsistence farming until tourism and retirement industries developed around Bull Shoals and Norfork Lakes.
county_metadata.research_tips: Baxter County was formed in 1873 and records at the County Clerk's office in Mountain Home are generally complete from that date. This Ozark county in north-central Arkansas had a predominantly Unionist population during the Civil War, and many families fled to Missouri. Check Missouri county records (particularly Howell and Oregon counties) for Baxter County families displaced during the war. The Baxter County Historical and Genealogical Society in Mountain Home maintains surname files and cemetery records. Mountain Home has become a retirement destination, so many recent residents have roots elsewhere. The White River and Norfork Lake areas are well-documented in local histories. GLO land patents are available online. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Baxter County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Mountain Home holds deed books from 1873

county_metadata.record_losses: 1862 Civil War damage during Battle of Pea Ridge campaign; some early records lost but many survived
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Benton County formed 1836 from Washington County. County seat is Bentonville. Located in the extreme northwest corner of Arkansas. Site of the Battle of Pea Ridge (1862), one of the most significant Civil War engagements west of the Mississippi. Now one of Arkansas's most populous counties, home to Walmart headquarters.
county_metadata.research_tips: Benton County in northwest Arkansas suffered Civil War damage during the Pea Ridge campaign (1862), and early records were partially destroyed. The County Clerk's office in Bentonville holds surviving records. Northwest Arkansas was a contested area during the Civil War with both Union and Confederate sympathies. Check Civil War pension records for both sides. The Pea Ridge National Military Park has research files related to the battle. The Benton County Historical Society and the Shiloh Museum of Ozark History in Springdale are excellent local resources. Cherokee removal (Trail of Tears) passed through this area -- check Cherokee Nation records at the Oklahoma Historical Society and NARA Fort Worth. This is now one of Arkansas's fastest-growing areas (Walmart headquarters in Bentonville, University of Arkansas in Fayetteville), but historical records reflect its Ozark frontier origins.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Benton County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Bentonville holds deed books from 1836

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Boone County formed 1869 from Carroll and Madison counties. County seat is Harrison. Located in the Ozark Mountains of northern Arkansas. Named for Daniel Boone's descendants who settled in the area. The county was strongly Unionist during the Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Boone County records at the County Clerk's office in Harrison are generally complete from 1869. This Ozark county was strongly Unionist during the Civil War, and guerrilla warfare devastated the area. Many families fled to Missouri during the conflict and returned after 1865. Check Harrison Civil War Union veteran records and Missouri county records for wartime displacement. The Boone County Heritage Museum in Harrison has local history collections. The county was named for Daniel Boone's descendants who settled in the area. Zinc mining records from the early 1900s document many families. The county's isolation in the Ozarks means church records from small Baptist and Methodist congregations are particularly important. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Boone County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Harrison holds deed books from 1869

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Bradley County formed 1840 from Union County. County seat is Warren. Located in the timber and cotton region of southeastern Arkansas. Named for Hugh Bradley, an early settler.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bradley County records are housed at the County Clerk's office in Warren, with good survival from 1840. Located in the timber and cotton region of southeast Arkansas, the county has strong probate records with detailed estate inventories listing enslaved persons. The Warren Public Library holds local history materials. Timber industry records from the late 1800s and early 1900s are important for tracking families in the lumber camps. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are essential for African American research. FamilySearch has indexed Bradley County marriages and other vital records. Check the Arkansas History Commission for microfilmed deed books. GLO land patents for original entries are available through BLM.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Bradley County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Warren holds deed books from 1840

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Calhoun County formed 1850 from Dallas, Ouachita, and Union counties. County seat is Hampton. One of Arkansas's smallest counties by population. The Smackover oil field partially extends into northern Calhoun County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Calhoun County is one of Arkansas's smallest counties, with records at the County Clerk's office in Hampton that are complete from its 1850 formation. This sparsely populated south-central county was a cotton and timber area. Probate records contain detailed estate inventories. The county's small population means fewer records overall, but those that survive are well-preserved. Oil discovery in the 1920s (Smackover field, partially in Calhoun County) brought a population boom documented in local newspapers and land records. The Arkansas History Commission has microfilmed Calhoun County records. Cemetery transcriptions by the South Arkansas Historical Foundation cover many family burial grounds.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Calhoun County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Hampton holds deed books from 1850

county_metadata.record_losses: 1869 courthouse fire at Carrollton destroyed most early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Carroll County formed 1833 from Izard County. County seat is Berryville. Located in the Ozark Mountains of northwest Arkansas. Has a unique dual-courthouse system with Berryville (western district) and Eureka Springs (eastern district). The 1869 courthouse fire at Carrollton destroyed most early records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Carroll County's 1869 courthouse fire at Carrollton destroyed most early records. The county seat is now Berryville, and records at the County Clerk's office begin primarily after 1869. The county has a unique dual-courthouse arrangement (Berryville in the west, Eureka Springs in the east). Carroll County was in the Ozark frontier zone with significant Cherokee interaction before removal. Eureka Springs, founded in 1879 as a health resort, attracted visitors from across the country and has separate municipal records. The Carroll County Historical and Genealogical Society maintains reconstructed records and family files. For pre-fire records, check adjacent county records (Washington, Madison, Boone) and GLO land patents. FamilySearch has post-fire county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Carroll County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Berryville holds deed books from 1843

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Chicot County formed 1823 from Arkansas County. County seat is Lake Village. Southernmost county in Arkansas, bordering Louisiana and Mississippi. Named for Point Chicot on the Mississippi River. Major plantation county with the largest enslaved population percentage in antebellum Arkansas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chicot County is the southernmost county in Arkansas and one of the oldest, with records at the County Clerk's office in Lake Village. Its location on the Mississippi River made it a major plantation area, and enslaved persons constituted the majority of the antebellum population. Estate inventories and probate records are exceptionally detailed for slaveholding families. Freedmen's Bureau records are extensive for Chicot County. The county's proximity to Mississippi and Louisiana means cross-state research is essential. French colonial records from the Arkansas Post region may relate to early Chicot County families. The Southeast Arkansas Regional Library in Monticello has some Chicot County materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Chicot County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Lake Village holds deed books from 1823

source_profiles.repository: Chicot County Clerk, Lake Village / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Chicot County; includes both white and, after 1865, African American marriages; Freedmen's Bureau also recorded marriages for formerly enslaved couples

source_profiles.source_name: Chicot County Federal Land Patents -- BLM / GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents (original entries) for Chicot County from the General Land Office; Arkansas is a public land state and most original land titles derive from federal patents; searchable by county and patentee name at BLM website

source_profiles.source_name: Chicot County Freedmen's Bureau Records -- NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Freedmen's Bureau records for Chicot County including labor contracts, marriage records, school reports, and complaints; essential for tracing African American families in the transition from slavery to freedom

source_profiles.source_name: Chicot County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / Arkansas Gravestone Project
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Arkansas Gravestone Project / Local genealogical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Chicot County churches and family burial grounds; Arkansas Gravestone Project and Find A Grave provide online access; local genealogical societies have published transcription volumes

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Clark County formed 1818 from Arkansas County as one of the original territorial counties. County seat is Arkadelphia. Named for William Clark of the Lewis and Clark Expedition. Home to Ouachita Baptist University and Henderson State University.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clark County records at the County Clerk's office in Arkadelphia are well-preserved from 1818. As one of the original territorial counties, Clark's records are among the oldest in the state. Arkadelphia was home to two colleges (Ouachita Baptist University and Henderson State University), and their archives hold local history materials. The county was named for William Clark of Lewis and Clark Expedition fame. Antoine Road, the first major road in Arkansas Territory, passed through Clark County. Probate records are particularly detailed. The Clark County Historical Society maintains research files. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Clark County records. Check the Arkansas History Commission for territorial-era documents.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Clark County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Arkadelphia holds deed books from 1818

source_profiles.source_name: Clark County Deed Books -- County Clerk / Arkansas History Commission
source_profiles.repository: Clark County Clerk, Arkadelphia / Arkansas History Commission, Little Rock
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Clark County recording land conveyances, mortgages, and property transfers; County Clerk's office is the primary repository; Arkansas History Commission holds microfilm copies
source_profiles.survival_notes: Deed books complete from 1818; housed at County Clerk's office in Arkadelphia
